subject: english
learning_objectives: Identify types of figures of speech

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A figure of speech is any departure from the literal (i.e ordinary) use of a word or phrase. Some of the commonest figures of speech which cut across genres are simile, metaphor, irony, paradox, euphemism, hyperbole, personification, litotes, oxymoron, allegory, alliteration, pun, rhetorical question, etc.

sections.heading: Simile
sections.content:
Indirect comparison using 'like' or 'as'.
Examples: Peter is as cunning as a snake
Jane is like an angel

sections.heading: Metaphor
sections.content:
Direct comparison without 'like' or 'as'.
Examples: Peter is a snake
Jane is an angel

sections.heading: Personification
sections.content:
Giving human qualities to non-living things.
Examples: The sun smiled at me
The trees are dancing in the wind

sections.heading: Euphemism
sections.content:
Presenting unpleasant things in a palatable way.
Examples: The old man has kicked the bucket
The king has joined his ancestors

sections.heading: Irony
sections.content:
Meaning opposite of literal words.
Examples: What a pretty state of affairs!
He jokingly describes the midget as 'very tall'

sections.heading: Paradox
sections.content:
Statement that seems absurd but contains truth.
Examples: Make haste slowly
Attack is the best form of defence

sections.heading: Pun
sections.content:
Play on words with similar sound but different meanings.
Examples: The drunkard was laid on his bier (beer/bier)
He is a soul administrator (sole/soul)

sections.heading: Onomatopoeia
sections.content:
Words imitating actual sounds.
Examples: hiss of a snake
splash of water
